pages.full_text: Founded in 654 by St. Phibert raided by Vikings 10-14 buildings by dukes of Normandy,
pages.notes: Page dominated by a glued-in colour postcard/photo of the Abbaye de Jumieges (labelled 'Abbaye de Jumièges / SEINE-MARITIME'). Below the photo is a handwritten caption: 'Founded in 654 by St. Phibert raided by Vikings 10-14 buildings by dukes of Normandy,' — 'St. Phibert' may be a phonetic rendering of Saint Philibert. The '10-14' likely refers to 10th–14th century buildings. No handwritten page number visible.

pages.full_text:
They have [sketch of roof overhang] one end of the roof overhanging [sketch] the walls so that they can work in the dry to load + unload the ends of the house/barn. The gardens are very pretty. Some orchards are of eating apples, but most are cider apples. Oct 22-24 is the big festival. We bought cider + apple jelly at one of the farms. It is sold as brut, dry, or demi-sec. It looks like champagne.
		We drove Routot, where the linen museum was shut. Simon shopped at Pont-Audomer. We went down the Risle estuary to Honfleur. Honfleur has a beautiful harbour + a very straight, uphill road leading out of it. Lots of campsites are shut, but finally at __Hennequeville__ we found "Le Chant des Oiseaux" * * * * with a marvellous view. We are on the edge of a steep slope down to the sea. We walk down steep steps to walk on the sandy beach near the gulls. To our left is Trouville, then Deauville, + far in the distance [illegible] Norman [illegible]
pages.notes: Continuation of a narrative about Normandy, France. The diarist describes Norman farmhouse architecture (roof overhangs), apple orchards and cider, then a drive through Routot (linen museum shut), shopping at Pont-Audomer, travelling down the Risle estuary to Honfleur, then finding a campsite 'Le Chant des Oiseaux' (four stars) at Hennequeville with sea views towards Trouville and Deauville. 'Oct 22-24' is given as a festival date but no year appears on this page; year cannot be confirmed from the content alone. The bottom of the page is partially obscured/cut off; last two lines are only partly legible. The small inline sketches appear to illustrate a roof overhang structure. 'Simon' is a person's name, not a place.
